description:
You are working as a mobile build-and-release engineer who has to prepare a very small, self-contained firewall configuration that later stages in the CI/CD pipeline will load with elevated privileges.  
Your goal is to create a pair of files inside the engineer’s working directory:

1. Directory that must exist  
   • /home/user/ci/ (create it if it does not already exist)

2. Executable script that will be invoked by the privileged build runner  
   • Full path : /home/user/ci/firewall_rules.sh  
   • Exact contents (including blank lines and *no* extra spacing) must be:

```
#!/bin/bash

iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
iptables -A INPUT -j DROP
```

   ➔ The script must be given execution permission (chmod + x).

3. Plain-text log that auditors will read  
   • Full path : /home/user/ci/firewall_rules.log  
   • Exact contents (each line must end with a single UNIX newline `\n`, no trailing spaces):

```
[RULE] iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
[RULE] iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT
[RULE] iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT
[RULE] iptables -A INPUT -j DROP
```

Output / verification requirements that the automated checker will look for:

A. The file /home/user/ci/firewall_rules.sh exists, is executable, and its byte-for-byte content is exactly as shown above.  
B. The file /home/user/ci/firewall_rules.log exists and its content is exactly the four “[RULE] …” lines shown above.  
C. Both files reside inside /home/user/ci/.

The work can be completed entirely with a handful of standard shell commands (mkdir, echo, printf, chmod, etc.). No root privileges are required because you are only writing files, **not** applying the rules to the live firewall.

When you’re done, display the contents of both files to confirm their correctness.
truth:
Expected final state for automated grading:

1. Directory  
   • Path: /home/user/ci  
   • Must exist and be readable.

2. /home/user/ci/firewall_rules.sh  
   • Permissions: mode 755 (rwxr-xr-x) or at minimum executable by the owner.  
   • Exact file content (including the trailing newline after the last line):

Line 1: #!/bin/bash\n  
Line 2: \n  
Line 3: iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT\n  
Line 4: iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT\n  
Line 5: iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT\n  
Line 6: iptables -A INPUT -j DROP\n

3. /home/user/ci/firewall_rules.log  
   • Permissions: any regular readable file.  
   • Exact file content (including the trailing newline after the last line):

Line 1: [RULE] iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT\n  
Line 2: [RULE] iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 8080 -j ACCEPT\n  
Line 3: [RULE] iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 443 -j ACCEPT\n  
Line 4: [RULE] iptables -A INPUT -j DROP\n
